meta.title: SF-SPADE-2: Dynamic Tables, Snowpark and Transformations

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates the following stream:

```sql
CREATE STREAM product_changes ON TABLE products APPEND_ONLY = TRUE;
```

A colleague then runs `UPDATE products SET price = price * 1.1 WHERE category = 'Electronics'`. What records, if any, will appear in `product_changes` after this UPDATE?

options.text: Both a DELETE row and an INSERT row for each updated product (the standard CDC representation)
Only the new (after) version of each updated product as an INSERT row
No records — append-only streams do not capture UPDATE or DELETE operations
An error is raised because UPDATE operations are not permitted on tables with append-only streams

questions.explanation: Append-only streams track only INSERT operations. UPDATE and DELETE DML on the underlying table is not captured in an append-only stream. This is by design — append-only streams are optimized for sources where rows are only ever added, and the efficiency gain comes precisely from not tracking row modifications. The UPDATE operation still succeeds on the base table (no error is raised, so D is wrong). Standard streams (not append-only) would capture the UPDATE as a DELETE+INSERT pair (A). Option B describes a hypothetical partial-capture that does not exist.
questions.reference: Append-Only Stream — Behavior with UPDATE/DELETE

questions.stem: What type of Snowflake stream must be used when tracking changes on an External Table?

options.text: Standard stream
Append-only stream
Insert-only stream
External tables cannot have streams

questions.explanation: External tables expose data from files in external stages (S3, GCS, Azure Blob) and can only detect new file arrivals — they cannot track updates or deletes within files. Therefore, only insert-only streams are supported on external tables. Standard streams (A) require row-level change tracking not available for external tables. Append-only streams (B) are for internal tables where only inserts happen. External tables do support streams, but only the insert-only type (D is wrong).
questions.reference: Insert-Only Streams on External Tables

questions.stem:
A data engineer builds a task DAG with a `FINALIZE` task that should run after all parallel branches complete. They define:

```sql
CREATE TASK finalize_load
  WAREHOUSE = 'LOAD_WH'
  AFTER branch_a_task, branch_b_task
AS
  CALL update_pipeline_status('COMPLETE');
```

When does `finalize_load` execute?

options.text: After `branch_a_task` completes, regardless of `branch_b_task` status
After BOTH `branch_a_task` AND `branch_b_task` have completed successfully
Simultaneously with `branch_a_task` and `branch_b_task` as a parallel branch
Only when `branch_a_task` succeeds and `branch_b_task` fails

questions.explanation: When a task lists multiple tasks in its `AFTER` clause, it acts as a join node in the DAG and will only execute after ALL listed predecessor tasks have completed successfully. This enables fan-in patterns where parallel branches are synchronized before a final step. If either predecessor fails, `finalize_load` does not run (the DAG short-circuits). Options A, C, and D all misrepresent the fan-in behavior.
questions.reference: Task DAG — Fan-In with Multiple AFTER Dependencies

questions.stem:
A data engineer notices that a task that processes a stream is running every 5 minutes but consistently produces no output rows. The stream has data. After investigation, they discover the task is defined with:

```sql
WHEN SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA('orders_stream')
```

but the stream is actually named `order_stream` (no 's'). What happens at runtime?

options.text: Snowflake raises a stream-not-found error and suspends the task after the configured failure threshold
`SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA()` returns FALSE for a non-existent stream, so the task body is skipped every run
`SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA()` returns NULL for a non-existent stream, which is treated as TRUE, so the task runs but queries an empty stream
Snowflake auto-corrects the stream name to the nearest matching stream at compile time

questions.explanation: `SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA()` returns FALSE (not an error) when the specified stream does not exist or the name is incorrect. This causes the task's `WHEN` condition to evaluate to FALSE, and the task body is skipped silently every run — which explains why the task runs but produces no output and no errors. This is a subtle but common bug. Option A (error + suspend) does not happen. Option C (NULL treated as TRUE) is wrong. Option D (auto-correct) does not exist.
questions.reference: SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA — Behavior with Non-Existent Stream

questions.stem: A data engineer creates a stream on a secure view. Select TWO constraints that apply to streams on views.

options.text: The view must be a secure view; standard views do not support streams
All tables referenced by the view must have change tracking enabled
The view can reference tables from different databases within the same account
Streams on views automatically become insert-only, regardless of the view's underlying table type

questions.explanation: Snowflake streams on views have two key constraints: (1) the view must be a SECURE view — streams cannot be created on standard (non-secure) views (A is correct); (2) change tracking must be enabled on all base tables referenced by the view, which Snowflake uses to propagate changes through the view (B is correct). The view must reference tables in the same database for stream support (C is wrong — cross-database view streams have limitations). Streams on views support standard stream types, not just insert-only (D is wrong).
questions.reference: Streams on Views — Requirements and Constraints

questions.stem: A near-real-time inventory pipeline uses a task scheduled every 1 minute to merge stream data into a Silver table. The warehouse is an X-Small. During peak hours, the merge takes 90 seconds to complete. What is the behavior of the scheduled task runs during this overlap?

options.text: Snowflake queues a new run while the current run is executing, and starts it immediately after the current run finishes
Snowflake skips any scheduled run that overlaps with a currently executing run; the next non-overlapping scheduled interval attempts to run
Both runs execute concurrently, which could cause duplicate rows in the Silver table
The task automatically upgrades to a larger warehouse to reduce execution time below the schedule interval

questions.explanation: Snowflake Tasks do not queue concurrent runs. If a task is still running when the next scheduled interval fires, that scheduled run is skipped. The task will attempt its next run at the subsequent scheduled interval. This prevents concurrent execution of the same task (which could cause data conflicts) but also means that if a task consistently runs longer than its schedule interval, it will effectively run less frequently than scheduled. Options A (queuing) and C (concurrent execution) do not reflect Snowflake's behavior. Option D (automatic warehouse scaling) does not happen for warehouse-backed tasks.
questions.reference: Task Scheduling — Behavior When Execution Exceeds Schedule Interval

questions.stem:
A data engineer wants to create a task using serverless compute that processes a stream. They write:

```sql
CREATE TASK process_events
  USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE = 'MEDIUM'
  SCHEDULE = 'USING CRON 0 * * * * UTC'
  WHEN SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA('event_stream')
AS
  INSERT INTO silver_events SELECT * FROM event_stream WHERE METADATA$ACTION = 'INSERT';
```

What does `USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE = 'MEDIUM'` control?

options.text: It sets the minimum warehouse size that Snowflake will scale down to during the task run
It provides Snowflake a hint for the initial compute size to allocate; Snowflake may automatically adjust based on task history
It creates a dedicated MEDIUM warehouse named after the task for exclusive use
It sets a hard cap on the compute size that Snowflake will allocate for this task

questions.explanation: `USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE` is a hint to Snowflake's serverless compute engine about what size to start with for the first run. Over time, Snowflake learns from execution history and may automatically scale the compute allocation up or down — the parameter is an initial suggestion, not a fixed constraint. It does not create a named warehouse (C), is not a minimum scale-down size (A), and is not a hard cap (D). Snowflake manages the actual compute provisioning dynamically in serverless mode.
questions.reference: Serverless Tasks — USER_TASK_MANAGED_INITIAL_WAREHOUSE_SIZE

questions.stem: A data engineer is auditing their task graph and runs `SELECT * FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TASK_HISTORY(SCHEDULED_TIME_RANGE_START => DATEADD('hours', -24, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP())))`. Select TWO columns in the result that are most useful for diagnosing why a task run failed.

options.text: `ERROR_CODE` and `ERROR_MESSAGE` columns that contain the failure reason
`RETURN_VALUE` column that contains the exception stack trace from the task body
`STATE` column that shows SUCCEEDED, FAILED, SKIPPED, or CANCELLED
`WAREHOUSE_EXTERNAL_SIZE` column that shows the compute used by the failed task

questions.explanation: `ERROR_CODE` and `ERROR_MESSAGE` directly describe what went wrong when a task fails (A is correct). `STATE` tells the engineer the outcome of each run and helps filter to only FAILED runs (C is correct). `RETURN_VALUE` is populated by stored procedures that explicitly return a value — it does not contain a stack trace for general task failures (B is wrong). `WAREHOUSE_EXTERNAL_SIZE` is not a column in `TASK_HISTORY` output (D is wrong).
questions.reference: TASK_HISTORY — Error Diagnosis Columns

questions.stem: A data engineer creates a Dynamic Table with `TARGET LAG = '10 minutes'`. The base table has not changed in 2 hours. How many refreshes will Snowflake execute during that 2-hour period?

options.text: 12 refreshes — one every 10 minutes regardless of whether base data changed
0 refreshes — Snowflake skips refreshes when base data has not changed to save compute
1 refresh — Snowflake runs exactly one refresh to confirm no changes and then waits
It depends on the refresh mode: FULL mode skips, INCREMENTAL mode always runs

questions.explanation: Snowflake's Dynamic Table refresh engine is change-aware. If the base tables have not changed, Snowflake can determine there is no new data to process and will skip the refresh entirely, saving compute costs. This is one of the advantages of Dynamic Tables over a naive scheduled task that runs regardless of data changes. Option A (always refreshing) is incorrect and would waste compute. Option C (exactly one) is wrong — Snowflake may run a lightweight check but skips the full computation. Option D incorrectly suggests mode-dependent skip behavior.
questions.reference: Dynamic Tables — Refresh Skipping When No Base Data Changes

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates the following Dynamic Table:

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE DYNAMIC TABLE customer_summary
  TARGET LAG = '30 minutes'
  WAREHOUSE = 'DT_WH'
AS
SELECT
  customer_id,
  COUNT(*) AS order_count,
  SUM(amount) AS total_spend,
  MAX(order_date) AS last_order_date
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id;
```

Snowflake chooses INCREMENTAL refresh for this table. A batch of 500 new orders for 200 distinct customers arrives. What does the incremental refresh process?

options.text: Recomputes `customer_summary` for all customers from scratch by re-executing the full SELECT
Computes the delta for only the 200 affected `customer_id` groups and merges those changes into `customer_summary`
Appends the 500 new order rows directly to `customer_summary` without aggregation
Detects the 200 changed customers but still re-aggregates all customers to ensure correctness

questions.explanation: Incremental refresh for Dynamic Tables processes only the rows/groups affected by the base table changes. For a GROUP BY aggregation, Snowflake identifies the affected `customer_id` keys (200 in this case), recomputes aggregates only for those groups, and updates (merges) those rows in the dynamic table. The remaining ~N-200 unchanged customers are untouched. This is far more efficient than a full refresh (A and D). Option C (appending raw rows) would break the aggregation semantic.
questions.reference: Dynamic Tables — Incremental Refresh for Aggregations

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE my_dt SUSPEND`. Select TWO things that are true while `my_dt` is suspended.

options.text: The dynamic table is no longer refreshed automatically
The data in `my_dt` at the time of suspension remains queryable
Downstream dynamic tables that reference `my_dt` are also automatically suspended
The dynamic table's definition is deleted and must be recreated with `ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE RESUME`

questions.explanation: When a Dynamic Table is suspended, automatic refreshes stop — no new data is written (A is correct). However, the existing data in the table at the time of suspension remains available for queries, it just becomes increasingly stale (B is correct). Snowflake does NOT automatically cascade the suspension to downstream dynamic tables (C is wrong — downstream tables may eventually become stale but are not automatically suspended). Suspension does not delete the table definition; `ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE RESUME` simply re-enables refreshes (D is wrong).
questions.reference: ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE SUSPEND — Behavior

questions.stem:
A data engineer defines a Dynamic Table using a non-deterministic function:

```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE DYNAMIC TABLE daily_snapshot
  TARGET LAG = '1 hour'
  WAREHOUSE = 'DT_WH'
AS
SELECT order_id, amount, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP() AS snapshot_time
FROM orders;
```

What refresh behavior should the engineer expect?

options.text: Snowflake will use INCREMENTAL refresh and call `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()` once per refresh to get a consistent timestamp for all new rows
The Dynamic Table will be forced to use FULL refresh mode because `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()` is non-deterministic and incremental refresh cannot reliably compute deltas containing it
The Dynamic Table will fail to create because non-deterministic functions are not allowed in Dynamic Table definitions
Snowflake will replace `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()` with the table's last refresh timestamp automatically

questions.explanation: Non-deterministic functions (like `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()`, `RANDOM()`, `SEQ4()`) produce different results each time they are evaluated, which breaks the incremental refresh engine's ability to compute reliable change deltas. Snowflake falls back to FULL refresh mode for queries containing non-deterministic functions. The table CAN be created (C is wrong) — Snowflake allows it but warns that INCREMENTAL refresh cannot be used. Snowflake does not modify query semantics (D) or make non-deterministic functions deterministic for incremental purposes (A).
questions.reference: Dynamic Tables — Incremental Refresh and Non-Deterministic Functions

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to manually trigger an immediate refresh of a Dynamic Table that normally has `TARGET LAG = '6 hours'`, without changing the lag setting. Which command achieves this?

options.text: `ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE my_dt SET TARGET LAG = '0 seconds'`
`ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE my_dt REFRESH`
`EXECUTE IMMEDIATE (SELECT * FROM my_dt)`
`CALL SYSTEM$REFRESH_DYNAMIC_TABLE('my_dt')`

questions.explanation: `ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE <name> REFRESH` triggers an immediate, on-demand refresh of the dynamic table without modifying its `TARGET LAG` setting. This is useful for forcing fresh data after a critical upstream change. Option A would change the lag setting, which affects ongoing scheduling. Option C just queries the table — it does not trigger a refresh. Option D is not a real Snowflake system call.
questions.reference: ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE REFRESH — Manual Trigger

questions.stem: How does a Dynamic Table differ from a Materialized View in Snowflake, specifically regarding supported SQL?

options.text: Materialized Views support JOINs across multiple tables; Dynamic Tables do not
Dynamic Tables support JOINs, aggregations across multiple tables, and self-referential patterns; Materialized Views are restricted to single-table queries with limited aggregations
Both support identical SQL; the only difference is that Dynamic Tables support lag-based scheduling
Dynamic Tables only support SELECT * without any transformations; Materialized Views support full SQL

questions.explanation: Dynamic Tables support a much richer SQL surface than Materialized Views. Dynamic Tables can JOIN multiple tables (including across schemas), perform complex aggregations, reference other Dynamic Tables as sources (chaining), and support GROUP BY, window functions, and more. Snowflake Materialized Views are more limited: they essentially support single-table queries with a restricted set of aggregations (no JOINs in Snowflake MVs). Option A has it backwards — it's MVs that are limited. Option C is wrong about parity. Option D is completely wrong about Dynamic Tables.
questions.reference: Dynamic Tables vs. Materialized Views — SQL Feature Comparison

questions.stem: A data engineer registers a Snowpark UDTF (User-Defined Table Function) using the `@udtf` decorator. What is the key difference between a UDTF and a scalar UDF in Snowpark?

options.text: A UDTF processes each row in parallel using pandas; a scalar UDF processes rows sequentially
A UDTF can return zero or more rows per input row; a scalar UDF returns exactly one scalar value per input row
A UDTF is always permanent and cannot be temporary; scalar UDFs can be temporary
A UDTF runs on the client machine; a scalar UDF runs inside Snowflake

questions.explanation: The fundamental difference: a scalar UDF returns exactly one value per input row (one-to-one mapping). A UDTF returns a table (zero or more rows) per input row (one-to-many mapping), making them useful for operations like parsing a JSON array into multiple rows, generating date ranges, or applying a model that produces multiple predictions per input. Both UDFs and UDTFs run inside Snowflake (D is wrong). The pandas/sequential distinction (A) describes vectorized vs. scalar UDFs, not UDTFs. Permanence is not a UDTF-specific restriction (C).
questions.reference: Snowpark UDTFs vs. Scalar UDFs

questions.stem:
A data engineer writes the following Snowpark code:

```python
df = session.table('sales')
df_enriched = df.join(
    session.table('customers'),
    df['customer_id'] == session.table('customers')['customer_id'],
    join_type='left'
)
df_enriched.write.mode('overwrite').save_as_table('enriched_sales')
```

Snowflake executes this as a single SQL statement. What SQL operation does this most closely translate to?

options.text: `CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE enriched_sales AS SELECT * FROM sales s LEFT JOIN customers c ON s.customer_id = c.customer_id`
`INSERT INTO enriched_sales SELECT * FROM sales s INNER JOIN customers c ON s.customer_id = c.customer_id`
`MERGE INTO enriched_sales USING (SELECT ...) ON (customer_id) WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE`
`COPY INTO enriched_sales FROM (SELECT * FROM sales s LEFT JOIN customers c ON s.customer_id = c.customer_id)`

questions.explanation: Snowpark's `df.write.mode('overwrite').save_as_table('enriched_sales')` compiles to a `CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE ... AS SELECT ...` (CTAS) statement with the full query plan (left join in this case) pushed down to Snowflake SQL. `mode('overwrite')` maps to `CREATE OR REPLACE`, which is equivalent to truncating and replacing. Option B uses INNER JOIN (wrong join type) and INSERT (not overwrite). Option C is a MERGE, which is not what `save_as_table` produces. Option D uses `COPY INTO` which is for stage-to-table loading, not DataFrame writes.
questions.reference: Snowpark DataFrame — SQL Translation of save_as_table

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to use third-party Python packages (e.g., `scikit-learn`, `numpy`) inside a Snowpark Python UDF. Select TWO requirements that must be met.

options.text: The packages must be available in the Snowflake Anaconda channel (or uploaded to a stage)
The packages must be specified in the UDF registration using the `packages` parameter
The packages must be installed on the Snowflake warehouse nodes using pip before the UDF runs
The packages must be bundled in a ZIP file and uploaded to a Snowflake internal stage

questions.explanation: Snowflake UDFs run in a sandboxed environment on warehouse nodes. To use third-party packages, they must either be available in Snowflake's curated Anaconda channel (or uploaded to a stage as a ZIP), and they must be declared at registration time via the `packages=['scikit-learn', 'numpy']` parameter so Snowflake can make them available in the sandbox (A and B are correct). You cannot `pip install` directly onto Snowflake warehouse nodes (C is wrong — the compute is managed and ephemeral). Option D (ZIP upload) is valid but is the alternative for packages NOT in Anaconda — it's not a universal requirement when the package IS in Anaconda.
questions.reference: Snowpark UDFs — Third-Party Package Dependencies

questions.stem: A data engineer writes a Snowpark stored procedure to perform a complex multi-step transformation and registers it with `EXECUTE AS CALLER`. A user with the `ANALYST` role calls the stored procedure. The procedure attempts to INSERT into a table that `ANALYST` does not have INSERT privilege on. What happens?

options.text: The INSERT succeeds because stored procedures always have elevated privileges
The INSERT fails with a permission error because `EXECUTE AS CALLER` runs the procedure with the CALLER's privileges (ANALYST role)
Snowflake prompts the ANALYST user to grant themselves INSERT privilege at runtime
The INSERT succeeds only if the stored procedure owner has INSERT privilege on the table

questions.explanation: `EXECUTE AS CALLER` is the rights model where all SQL within the stored procedure runs with the calling user's current role's privileges. Since `ANALYST` lacks INSERT on the target table, the INSERT fails. This contrasts with `EXECUTE AS OWNER`, where the procedure's owner role privileges apply instead. Option A is wrong — stored procedures do not auto-elevate privileges. Option C does not reflect Snowflake's runtime behavior. Option D describes `EXECUTE AS OWNER` behavior, not `CALLER`.
questions.reference: Stored Procedures — EXECUTE AS CALLER Privilege Model

questions.stem: A data engineer builds a Snowpark UDTF that parses a JSON string column into multiple event rows. The `process()` method yields individual rows. They also implement `end_partition()`. When is `end_partition()` called?

options.text: After every row is processed by `process()`, as a row-level post-processing hook
Once, after all rows in a partition have been processed by `process()`, allowing partition-level aggregation or finalization
Only when `process()` raises an exception, as an error handler
Before `process()` is called, to initialize partition-level state

questions.explanation: In Snowpark UDTFs, `process()` is called once per input row and can yield rows immediately. `end_partition()` is called once per partition after ALL rows in that partition have been processed by `process()`. This makes it ideal for finalizing partition-level state — for example, emitting a summary row, flushing accumulated state, or computing an aggregate across all rows in the partition before yielding the final output. It is not per-row (A), not an error handler (C), and not called before `process()` (D — that's what `__init__` is for).
questions.reference: Snowpark UDTFs — process() and end_partition() Methods

questions.stem:
A data engineer writes:

```python
df = session.sql('SELECT * FROM raw_events WHERE event_date = CURRENT_DATE()')
df.write.mode('append').save_as_table('daily_events')
```

Is this idiomatic Snowpark? What concern does this raise?

options.text: It is fully idiomatic; mixing `session.sql()` and DataFrame writes is the recommended pattern
It works but bypasses Snowpark's type-safe column API and column pushdown optimization; `session.table().filter()` would be preferred
It will fail because `session.sql()` returns a ResultSet, not a DataFrame, and `write` is not available on ResultSets
It will fail because `CURRENT_DATE()` is a SQL function and cannot be evaluated inside a Snowpark DataFrame context

questions.explanation: `session.sql()` returns a Snowpark DataFrame, so chaining `.write.save_as_table()` is technically valid (C is wrong). However, it is generally less idiomatic than using the DataFrame API (`session.table().filter(col('event_date') == current_date())`) because raw SQL strings bypass Snowpark's ability to introspect and validate column types at the Python level. Both approaches push computation to Snowflake (D is wrong — SQL functions work fine). Option A overstates that it's 'recommended' — it works but is not best practice for code that benefits from type safety and refactoring.
questions.reference: Snowpark — session.sql() vs. DataFrame API

questions.stem: A data engineer uses a Snowpark stored procedure to orchestrate a pipeline that creates a temp table, populates it, and then reads it back. The procedure is registered as a permanent stored procedure. Another session calls the same procedure simultaneously. What happens to the temp tables created inside the procedure?

options.text: Both sessions share the same temp table, causing data contamination between runs
Each session call gets its own session-scoped temp table, isolating the two executions
The second call fails because temp tables inside stored procedures are exclusive to the first caller
Temp tables inside stored procedures are actually permanent tables with a temp-like TTL of 1 hour

questions.explanation: In Snowflake, session-scoped temporary tables are isolated per session. When two sessions call the same stored procedure simultaneously, each session creates its own instance of any temp tables defined inside the procedure. They do not share temp table state, which ensures isolation and correctness. Option A (shared state) would be a serious data integrity problem and is not the case. Option C is wrong — multiple concurrent calls are fully supported. Option D misrepresents how temp tables work.
questions.reference: Snowpark Stored Procedures — Session-Scoped Temp Table Isolation

questions.stem: A data engineer is designing an ELT pipeline where raw JSON data is loaded into a Snowflake internal stage and then into a Bronze table. Which approach represents the ELT pattern?

options.text: Parse and normalize the JSON in a Spark job before writing to Snowflake
Load the raw JSON into Snowflake as a VARIANT column and perform all transformations using Snowflake SQL after landing
Transform the JSON into Parquet format using an AWS Lambda before staging in S3
Use a custom connector to transform JSON to CSV during the Snowflake load process

questions.explanation: ELT (Extract, Load, Transform) means loading data first into the target system and performing transformations inside the target platform. Snowflake's native VARIANT type is designed for storing raw semi-structured data like JSON, and Snowflake SQL (including FLATTEN, PARSE_JSON, lateral views) is used for in-place transformation. Options A, C, and D all describe ETL — transforming data BEFORE or DURING loading into Snowflake, outside of Snowflake's compute. The ELT preference with Snowflake is to minimize movement and leverage Snowflake's native compute for transformation.
questions.reference: ELT vs. ETL in Snowflake — Raw JSON Loading Pattern

questions.stem: A data engineering team uses a Bronze/Silver/Gold pattern with Streams and Tasks. `BRONZE_STREAM` on the `BRONZE_ORDERS` table feeds `SILVER_LOAD_TASK`. The Silver table then has its own stream `SILVER_STREAM` that feeds `GOLD_LOAD_TASK`. The engineer wants the Gold table to be updated only after the Silver table is fully updated. How should the task dependencies be structured?

options.text: `GOLD_LOAD_TASK` should run on its own schedule (every 10 minutes) independent of `SILVER_LOAD_TASK`
`GOLD_LOAD_TASK` should be defined as `AFTER SILVER_LOAD_TASK` in the same task DAG, so Gold processing only starts after Silver completes
Both tasks should share the same `WHEN` clause, pointing to `BRONZE_STREAM`
The `GOLD_LOAD_TASK` should poll `SILVER_STREAM` using `SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA()` on its own independent 1-minute schedule

questions.explanation: The correct approach is to chain the tasks in a single DAG: `SILVER_LOAD_TASK` as the root task (consuming BRONZE_STREAM), and `GOLD_LOAD_TASK` as `AFTER SILVER_LOAD_TASK` with `WHEN SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA('SILVER_STREAM')`. This ensures Gold processing only begins after Silver has been fully updated. Independent scheduling (A and D) creates a race condition where Gold might read Silver before it's updated. Sharing the Bronze stream's `WHEN` clause (C) would mean Gold runs on Bronze stream data, not Silver stream data.
questions.reference: Multi-Layer Pipeline — Task DAG Chaining with Streams

questions.stem: A team is implementing a dead-letter pattern for a Snowflake ELT pipeline where some records fail data quality checks during the Silver load. Select TWO characteristics of a well-designed dead-letter pattern in Snowflake.

options.text: Failed records are routed to a separate `DEAD_LETTER` table with the error reason and original record payload preserved
The main pipeline continues processing valid records even when some records fail validation
The task is configured with `ON_ERROR = ABORT_STATEMENT` so all records are rejected if any fail
Dead-letter records are permanently deleted after 24 hours to prevent the table from growing

questions.explanation: A dead-letter pattern's purpose is exactly to isolate bad records while allowing the good-record pipeline to continue. Routing failed records to a dedicated table with their error context (A) enables investigation and reprocessing without blocking the main pipeline. The main pipeline should continue processing valid records (B), which is the whole point of a dead-letter queue. `ON_ERROR = ABORT_STATEMENT` (C) is the opposite of a dead-letter pattern — it halts the entire load on any error. Dead-letter records should be retained for investigation and possible reprocessing, not auto-deleted (D).
questions.reference: Dead-Letter Pattern for ELT Error Handling in Snowflake

questions.stem: A data engineer is designing a pipeline where Snowflake is the downstream consumer of a Debezium CDC feed from a PostgreSQL database. Debezium publishes full row images with operation type (`c` for create, `u` for update, `d` for delete) to Kafka, which is then landed in a Snowflake Bronze table. Which Snowflake DML best propagates these CDC events from Bronze to a Silver target table?

options.text: Use a standard Snowflake stream on the Bronze table — Snowflake will automatically interpret Debezium's `op` field as stream metadata

Use `MERGE INTO` on the Silver table, joining on primary key, routing `c`/`u` operations to `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE` or `WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT`, and `d` operations to `WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE`
Use `INSERT INTO silver SELECT * FROM bronze_stream` and rely on Snowflake to detect duplicates automatically
Use the Snowflake stream on the Bronze table and filter by `METADATA$ACTION = 'DELETE'` for Debezium delete events

questions.explanation: When the upstream CDC system (Debezium) sends full-row images with an explicit operation field (`op`), the Bronze table just contains rows — it's the downstream pipeline's job to interpret the `op` field. A `MERGE INTO` on the Silver table using the Debezium `op` field to route inserts/updates vs. deletes is the correct pattern. Option A is wrong — Snowflake streams are agnostic to Debezium's payload; a stream on Bronze would show Debezium messages as INSERTs (rows landing in Bronze), not as the CDC operations they represent. Option C ignores deletes. Option D confuses Snowflake's native stream metadata with Debezium's payload fields.
questions.reference: Debezium CDC Integration with Snowflake — MERGE-Based Propagation

questions.stem: A data engineering team is evaluating Snowflake Tasks versus an external orchestrator (Apache Airflow) for a new pipeline. Which of the following is the BEST reason to use Snowflake Tasks instead of Airflow for this pipeline?

options.text: The pipeline needs to trigger a Salesforce API call after each successful Snowflake load
The pipeline is entirely within Snowflake (stream → transformation → Silver table) and the team wants to minimize external dependencies and operational overhead
The pipeline has complex branching logic that depends on data from multiple cloud systems
The pipeline needs SLA alerting via PagerDuty when tasks exceed their expected duration

questions.explanation: Snowflake Tasks are the best choice when the entire pipeline is within Snowflake and the team wants no external infrastructure to maintain. Tasks eliminate the need for an Airflow deployment, Airflow workers, and the associated ops overhead. Scenarios A (Salesforce API), C (multi-cloud branching), and D (PagerDuty integration) all require external system integration — exactly where Airflow excels but Snowflake Tasks cannot natively reach.
questions.reference: Snowflake Tasks vs. Airflow — Selection Criteria

questions.stem:
A data engineer uses `COPY INTO` with an inline transformation:

```sql
COPY INTO raw.orders (order_id, customer_id, amount_usd, loaded_at)
FROM (
  SELECT
    $1::INTEGER,
    $2::VARCHAR(50),
    $3::FLOAT * 1.08,
    CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()
  FROM @orders_stage/orders_20240601.csv.gz
)
FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = CSV SKIP_HEADER = 1);
```

What does the `$1`, `$2`, `$3` notation refer to in this context?

options.text: Named bind parameters that must be declared before the COPY INTO statement
Column position references in the source file — `$1` is the first column, `$2` the second, etc.
References to Snowflake session variables that hold the column values
References to columns in the target `raw.orders` table by position

questions.explanation: In a `COPY INTO` SELECT transformation, `$1`, `$2`, `$3`, etc. are positional column references to the columns of the source file being loaded. `$1` refers to the first column in the CSV, `$2` to the second, and so on. This allows you to reorder columns, cast types, or transform values from the file during load. They are not bind parameters (A), session variables (C), or references to the target table's columns (D).
questions.reference: COPY INTO — Inline Transformation with Positional Column References

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to implement a multi-table routing pattern where rows from a single staging table are routed to different target tables based on a `record_type` column. Select TWO Snowflake SQL approaches that support conditional multi-table inserts.

options.text: `INSERT ALL` with `WHEN condition THEN INTO table_name` clauses for each target table
`INSERT FIRST` with `WHEN condition THEN INTO table_name`, stopping at the first matching condition per row
`MERGE INTO` with multiple `WHEN MATCHED` clauses targeting different tables in a single statement
`COPY INTO` with a `ROUTING` parameter to send rows to named target tables

questions.explanation: Snowflake supports two conditional multi-table INSERT forms: `INSERT ALL` evaluates ALL `WHEN` conditions for each row, so a row matching multiple conditions inserts into multiple target tables (A). `INSERT FIRST` evaluates conditions in order and inserts the row into only the FIRST matching target table (B). `MERGE INTO` (C) targets a single table per statement — you cannot target different tables in one MERGE. `COPY INTO` (D) has no such routing parameter.
questions.reference: INSERT ALL / INSERT FIRST — Conditional Multi-Table Routing

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to perform a full sync of a `PRODUCTS` dimension table from a staging area. Products that exist in staging but not in the target should be inserted. Products that exist in both should be updated. Products that exist in the target but NOT in staging should be deleted. Which MERGE statement skeleton achieves this in Snowflake?

options.text: `MERGE INTO products USING staging ON (products.id = staging.id) WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE ... WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ...`
`MERGE INTO products USING staging ON (products.id = staging.id) WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE ... WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ... WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE`
`MERGE INTO products USING staging ON (products.id = staging.id) WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ...`
`MERGE INTO products USING staging ON (products.id = staging.id) WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN DELETE WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ...`

questions.explanation: A full-sync MERGE requires all three clauses: `WHEN MATCHED` (row in both source and target → UPDATE), `WHEN NOT MATCHED` (row in source but not in target → INSERT), and `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE` (row in target but not in source → DELETE). Option A misses the delete for orphaned target rows. Option C deletes matching rows instead of updating them. Option D uses `NOT MATCHED BY TARGET` for DELETE, which is semantically backwards — `NOT MATCHED BY TARGET` means 'in source but not target' → that's for INSERTs.
questions.reference: Full-Sync MERGE — WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE

questions.stem: A data engineer runs a heavy ELT query that joins a 10TB fact table with a 1GB dimension table and applies several aggregations. The query is run nightly on an X-Large warehouse. The engineer notices that roughly 5% of runs take 10x longer than typical runs, seemingly at random. Which Snowflake feature is MOST appropriate to address these outlier query performance spikes?

options.text: Enable result caching on the warehouse so repeated queries are served from cache
Enable the Query Acceleration Service on the warehouse to automatically scale compute for outlier queries
Switch to a 2X-Large warehouse to ensure consistent performance
Use a Multi-Cluster Warehouse to handle concurrent query load

questions.explanation: The Query Acceleration Service (QAS) is specifically designed for outlier query situations — queries that occasionally take much longer than typical due to data skew, large scan partitions, or other variability. QAS automatically provides additional elastic compute for these outlier queries without requiring the entire warehouse to be upsized. Result caching (A) only helps if the exact same query with the same data is re-run. A permanently larger warehouse (C) costs more 100% of the time to fix a 5% problem. Multi-Cluster Warehouses (D) address concurrent user/query load, not single-query performance outliers.
questions.reference: Query Acceleration Service — Outlier Query Performance

questions.stem: A data engineer sets up a Snowpipe to auto-ingest files from an S3 bucket. After creating the Snowpipe, how does Snowflake receive notification that new files have arrived in S3?

options.text: Snowflake polls the S3 bucket on a configurable interval (default: 1 minute)
S3 sends an event notification to an SQS queue, and Snowflake's Snowpipe service reads from that SQS queue to trigger ingestion
The data engineer must call `ALTER PIPE ... REFRESH` each time new files arrive
A Snowflake Task polls `SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS()` and triggers ingestion when new files are detected

questions.explanation: Snowpipe event-driven ingestion from S3 works via S3 Event Notifications → SQS → Snowflake. When a new object arrives in the configured S3 bucket/prefix, S3 publishes an event to an SQS queue. Snowflake's Snowpipe service continuously reads from that SQS queue and triggers file loading within seconds to minutes. Snowflake does not poll S3 directly (A). Manual `REFRESH` (C) is the fallback for ad-hoc loads, not the auto-ingest mechanism. There is no polling task (D).
questions.reference: Snowpipe Auto-Ingest — S3 Event Notification via SQS

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to check the credit consumption of a Snowpipe pipe over the last 7 days, broken down by hour. Which function provides this billing/usage data?

options.text: `COPY_HISTORY(TABLE_NAME => 'target_table', START_TIME => DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()))`
`PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY(DATE_RANGE_START => DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()))`
`SHOW PIPES` with the `CREDITS_USED` column
`INFORMATION_SCHEMA.SNOWPIPE_HISTORY` view

questions.explanation: `PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY()` is the table function that returns credit usage for Snowpipe over a specified time range, broken down by pipe and time interval. It shows credits consumed for auto-ingest compute. `COPY_HISTORY()` (A) shows file-level load status, not credit consumption. `SHOW PIPES` (C) shows configuration, not usage metrics. `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.SNOWPIPE_HISTORY` (D) is not a real Snowflake view.
questions.reference: PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY — Snowpipe Credit Consumption

questions.stem: A data engineer is building a pipeline that calls Snowflake's REST SQL API from a microservice. The microservice must authenticate using key-pair (JWT) authentication. After generating the JWT, which HTTP header carries the token in REST API calls to Snowflake?

options.text: `X-Snowflake-Token: Bearer <jwt>`
`Authorization: Snowflake Token="<jwt>"`
`Authorization: Bearer <jwt>`
`X-Snowflake-Authorization-Token-Type: KEYPAIR_JWT` with the JWT in the `Authorization: Bearer` header

questions.explanation: Snowflake's REST SQL API with key-pair JWT authentication requires TWO headers: `Authorization: Bearer <jwt>` containing the signed JWT, AND `X-Snowflake-Authorization-Token-Type: KEYPAIR_JWT` which tells Snowflake to validate the token as a key-pair JWT (not an OAuth token). Without the token-type header, Snowflake cannot distinguish JWT from OAuth Bearer tokens. Option C alone is incomplete — it's missing the token type header. Options A and B use non-standard or incorrect header formats.
questions.reference: Snowflake REST API — JWT Authentication Headers

questions.stem: A data engineer uses the Snowpipe Streaming API (`insertRows()`) to push data into Snowflake. The SDK client buffers rows into a channel before flushing to Snowflake. What is a Snowpipe Streaming 'channel' in this context?

options.text: A named Kafka consumer group that reads from a Kafka topic and writes to Snowflake
A logical, ordered sequence of rows for a specific table partition, managed by the SDK client, that provides exactly-once delivery guarantees via offset tracking
A Snowflake internal stage that temporarily holds rows before they are committed to the target table
A serverless function that transforms rows before inserting them into the target table

questions.explanation: In Snowpipe Streaming, a 'channel' is a logical unit of ordered data flow for a specific target table. Each channel tracks an offset (similar to a Kafka consumer offset) to provide exactly-once semantics — if the client fails and restarts, it can resume from the last committed offset without duplicating rows. Multiple channels can write to the same table in parallel. Channels are not Kafka consumer groups (A), not internal stages (C), and not transformation functions (D).
questions.reference: Snowpipe Streaming — Channels and Offset Tracking

questions.stem: A data engineer is reviewing the observability setup for a Snowflake ELT pipeline. Select TWO Snowflake-native mechanisms that provide task run history including execution duration and error messages.

options.text: `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TASK_HISTORY()` table function with a time range filter
`SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TASK_HISTORY` view (with up to 45 minutes of latency)
`SHOW TASK RUNS` command for real-time task run status
`SYSTEM$TASK_HISTORY()` function filtered by task name

questions.explanation: Two valid Snowflake-native mechanisms: (A) `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TASK_HISTORY()` is a table function available with low latency (within minutes) and is the primary operational tool for querying recent task runs; (B) `SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TASK_HISTORY` is a long-retention view (up to 365 days of history) with up to 45-minute latency, suitable for trend analysis. Option C (`SHOW TASK RUNS`) is not a valid Snowflake command. Option D (`SYSTEM$TASK_HISTORY()`) is not the correct function name — the correct function is in `INFORMATION_SCHEMA`.
questions.reference: Task History — INFORMATION_SCHEMA vs. ACCOUNT_USAGE

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `ALTER TASK my_task SUSPEND` and then `ALTER TASK my_task RESUME`. What is the net effect on the task's schedule?

options.text: The task is permanently disabled until a `CREATE OR REPLACE TASK` is issued
The task resumes its configured schedule as if it had never been suspended — no schedule changes occur
The task's schedule is reset to run immediately on resume, then continues the configured interval
Suspending and then resuming a task doubles the schedule interval as a backoff penalty

questions.explanation: `ALTER TASK ... SUSPEND` stops the task from being scheduled. `ALTER TASK ... RESUME` re-activates the task on its configured schedule from that point forward. The schedule definition (CRON or INTERVAL) is preserved during suspension and resumes exactly as configured. No immediate run is triggered on resume (C), no doubling occurs (D), and the task definition is not deleted (A).
questions.reference: ALTER TASK SUSPEND / RESUME — Schedule Behavior

questions.stem: A data engineer has a multi-task DAG: ROOT → [CHILD_A, CHILD_B] → FINALIZE. The team needs to add a new task CHILD_C that must run after ROOT and before FINALIZE. What is the CORRECT order of DDL operations to modify a live DAG safely?

options.text: 1. Create CHILD_C AFTER ROOT, 2. Alter FINALIZE to add CHILD_C to its AFTER clause, 3. Resume CHILD_C
1. Suspend ROOT, 2. Create CHILD_C AFTER ROOT, 3. Alter FINALIZE to add CHILD_C to its AFTER clause, 4. Resume CHILD_C, 5. Resume ROOT
1. Drop and recreate the entire DAG with CHILD_C included
1. Suspend FINALIZE, 2. Create CHILD_C, 3. Resume FINALIZE, 4. Resume ROOT

questions.explanation: Modifying a Snowflake Task DAG requires suspending the root task first to prevent the DAG from running during structural changes. The correct sequence for adding CHILD_C is: (1) Suspend ROOT to freeze the DAG, (2) Create CHILD_C with `AFTER ROOT`, (3) Alter FINALIZE's AFTER clause to include CHILD_C (so FINALIZE waits for A, B, and C), (4) Resume CHILD_C, (5) Resume ROOT to re-activate the DAG. Option A skips the root suspension, risking a DAG run during modification. Options C and D are either overly destructive or structurally incorrect.
questions.reference: Task DAG Modification — Safe Suspend-Modify-Resume Pattern

options.text: The INSERT is committed and the DELETE failure is logged but the task continues
The entire block is rolled back because the transaction is not committed until the COMMIT statement at the end
The INSERT is committed because Snowflake auto-commits each DML statement by default inside stored procedures
The task fails and Snowflake automatically retries only the DELETE statement

questions.explanation: In Snowflake scripting blocks, statements between `BEGIN` and `COMMIT` are part of a single transaction. If any statement fails before `COMMIT`, Snowflake automatically rolls back all preceding statements in the block — the INSERT is rolled back along with the failed DELETE. This ensures atomicity. Option A (partial commit) violates transactional semantics. Option C (auto-commit per statement) is not the behavior inside explicit transaction blocks — auto-commit only applies outside explicit `BEGIN`/`COMMIT` blocks. Option D (partial retry) is not a Snowflake feature.
questions.reference: Snowflake Scripting — BEGIN/COMMIT Transaction Atomicity

questions.stem: A data engineer configures a task with `ERROR_INTEGRATION = my_notification_integration`. Select TWO things the error integration can notify when the task fails.

options.text: The task name and schema that failed
The full ERROR_MESSAGE and ERROR_CODE from the failed run
The specific SQL statement within the task body that caused the failure
A recommendation for which warehouse size to use to avoid the failure

questions.explanation: When a task fails with an `ERROR_INTEGRATION` configured, Snowflake sends a JSON payload to the configured notification endpoint (SNS, Azure Event Grid, etc.) containing metadata about the failure including: the task name, schema/database, scheduled time, error code, and error message. Options A and B are included in the notification payload. Option C (specific SQL line within the task body) is generally not included in the notification payload — error messages describe the overall failure, not precise line numbers within multi-statement bodies. Option D (warehouse size recommendation) is not part of Snowflake's notification schema.
questions.reference: Task ERROR_INTEGRATION — Notification Payload Contents

questions.stem: A data engineer's task DAG has CHILD_A and CHILD_B running in parallel after ROOT. CHILD_A succeeds. CHILD_B fails. What happens to FINALIZE (which has `AFTER CHILD_A, CHILD_B`)?

options.text: FINALIZE runs after CHILD_A succeeds, ignoring CHILD_B's failure
FINALIZE does not run because one of its predecessors (CHILD_B) failed
FINALIZE runs but receives the failure status from CHILD_B as an input parameter
FINALIZE is suspended automatically when a predecessor fails

questions.explanation: In a Snowflake Task DAG, a task with multiple predecessors in its `AFTER` clause (fan-in) only runs if ALL predecessors have completed SUCCESSFULLY. If any predecessor fails (CHILD_B in this case), the downstream task (FINALIZE) is not triggered. The DAG short-circuits at the failure point. FINALIZE is not automatically suspended (D) — it simply doesn't run for this DAG execution. It will attempt to run again in the next root task cycle. Options A and C misrepresent Snowflake's fan-in behavior.
questions.reference: Task DAG Fan-In — Behavior When One Predecessor Fails

questions.stem: A task uses a `WHEN` clause with a complex expression including `SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA()`. A data engineer wants to see how often this condition evaluates to FALSE (causing SKIPPED runs) vs TRUE (causing execution) over the past week. Which approach retrieves this information?

options.text: Query `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TASK_HISTORY()` and count rows where `STATE = 'SKIPPED'` vs `STATE = 'SUCCEEDED'` or `'FAILED'`
Query `SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA()` for the past week's historical values
Query `SHOW STREAMS` to see how many times the stream was read vs skipped
Enable `TASK_EXECUTION_LOGGING = TRUE` on the task to write WHEN clause evaluations to a log table

questions.explanation: `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TASK_HISTORY()` records every scheduled task attempt with its `STATE` — SKIPPED, SUCCEEDED, or FAILED. Querying and counting by STATE over the past 7 days is the correct way to analyze WHEN clause evaluation frequency. `SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA()` (B) is a runtime function that returns current state, not a historical log. `SHOW STREAMS` (C) shows stream configuration, not evaluation history. Option D uses a non-existent `TASK_EXECUTION_LOGGING` property.
questions.reference: Task History — Analyzing SKIPPED vs. SUCCEEDED Run Rates

options.text: `orders_stream_audit` is also advanced to the same offset as `orders_stream_silver`
`orders_stream_audit` still contains the unconsumed records because each stream maintains its own independent offset
`orders_stream_audit` is automatically emptied as a side effect of the other stream's consumption
`orders_stream_audit` raises a stale error because the underlying data was consumed by the other stream

questions.explanation: Each Snowflake stream on the same table maintains its own independent offset pointer into the table's change-tracking metadata. Consuming (advancing) one stream does not affect the offset of other streams on the same table. `orders_stream_audit` retains all unconsumed records even after `orders_stream_silver` has been consumed and committed. This is the correct design for fan-out patterns where multiple consumers need to independently process the same change data.
questions.reference: Multiple Streams on Same Table — Independent Offsets

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to determine whether a Dynamic Table `ORDERS_GOLD` is currently set to automatically refresh or has been manually suspended. Which command provides this status most directly?

options.text: `SELECT * FROM DYNAMIC_TABLE_REFRESH_HISTORY(NAME => 'ORDERS_GOLD') LIMIT 1`
`SHOW DYNAMIC TABLES LIKE 'ORDERS_GOLD'` — check the `SCHEDULING_STATE` column
`DESCRIBE DYNAMIC TABLE ORDERS_GOLD` — check the `STATE` field
`SELECT IS_SUSPENDED FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.DYNAMIC_TABLES WHERE TABLE_NAME = 'ORDERS_GOLD'`

questions.explanation: `SHOW DYNAMIC TABLES LIKE 'ORDERS_GOLD'` returns a row per matching dynamic table with a `SCHEDULING_STATE` column that shows `ACTIVE` (automatic refresh is enabled) or `SUSPENDED` (refresh is paused). This is the most direct way to check the operational state of a dynamic table. `DYNAMIC_TABLE_REFRESH_HISTORY()` (A) shows past refreshes but not current scheduling state. `DESCRIBE DYNAMIC TABLE` (C) shows the definition but not the live scheduling state. Option D references a non-existent `IS_SUSPENDED` column in that view.
questions.reference: SHOW DYNAMIC TABLES — SCHEDULING_STATE Column

questions.stem:
A dynamic table `ORDERS_SILVER` has UDFs referenced in its query definition:

```sql
CREATE DYNAMIC TABLE orders_silver
  TARGET LAG = '10 minutes'
  WAREHOUSE = 'DT_WH'
AS
SELECT order_id, clean_phone(phone_number) AS phone, amount
FROM raw_orders;
```

Where `clean_phone` is a Python UDF. Is this supported in a dynamic table?

options.text: Yes, dynamic tables support UDFs in their query definition, but UDFs cause the dynamic table to use FULL refresh mode
No, dynamic tables do not support UDFs of any kind in their query definition
Yes, dynamic tables support both SQL and Python UDFs, and the UDF is executed on each refresh (incremental or full)
Yes, but only SQL UDFs are supported; Python UDFs are not allowed in dynamic table definitions

questions.explanation: Dynamic Tables do support UDFs (including Python UDFs) in their query definitions. However, UDFs are considered non-deterministic from the perspective of Snowflake's incremental refresh engine — the engine cannot track which UDF invocations produce changed outputs without re-running the UDF. As a result, dynamic tables that reference UDFs are forced into FULL refresh mode. They still work correctly — just not incrementally. Option B is wrong (UDFs are supported). Option C is wrong (incremental refresh is not available with UDFs). Option D is wrong (Python UDFs are allowed, but cause full refresh).
questions.reference: Dynamic Tables — UDFs Force FULL Refresh Mode

questions.stem: A dynamic table chain has three levels: DT_A → DT_B → DT_C. DT_C has `TARGET LAG = '1 hour'`. DT_B has `TARGET LAG = DOWNSTREAM`. DT_A has a fixed `TARGET LAG = '6 hours'`. A user queries DT_C. The data in DT_A was last refreshed 5 hours ago, DT_B was refreshed 50 minutes ago. Is DT_C within its lag target?

options.text: No — DT_C's data is 5 hours stale because it depends on DT_A which is 5 hours old
Yes — DT_C was refreshed 50 minutes ago via DT_B, so it is within its 1-hour lag target
It depends — if DT_A had no new data in 5 hours, then DT_C is current; if DT_A had new data, DT_C may be stale
Indeterminate — DT_C's lag target conflicts with DT_A's 6-hour fixed lag

questions.explanation: The key insight is whether DT_A actually had new data since its last refresh. If DT_A's source (raw tables) had no changes in 5 hours, then DT_A is fully current despite not having refreshed recently — there was nothing new to refresh. DT_B would have refreshed and found no changes from DT_A, and DT_C at 50 minutes is within its 1-hour target. However, if DT_A's source had changes that haven't been incorporated yet (because DT_A won't refresh for another hour due to its 6-hour lag), then DT_C does not reflect those latest changes and is technically stale relative to the source. The answer depends on whether upstream data changed.
questions.reference: Dynamic Table Chain Lag — Propagation Through Mixed Lag Settings

questions.stem: A data engineer reviews `DYNAMIC_TABLE_REFRESH_HISTORY()` and finds a row with `STATE = 'FAILED'` and `ERROR_MESSAGE` indicating an out-of-memory error during a FULL refresh. Select TWO remediation strategies.

options.text: Increase the warehouse size assigned to the dynamic table's `WAREHOUSE` parameter
Rewrite the dynamic table query to eliminate features that prevent incremental refresh (such as LIMIT or non-deterministic functions) so incremental refresh can be used instead
Add a `MAX_DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0` property to the dynamic table to free up memory
Drop the dynamic table and re-create it with a Streams+Tasks pattern instead

questions.explanation: Two valid remediations for OOM during FULL refresh: (A) Use a larger warehouse — more memory per node directly addresses OOM. (B) Eliminate query constructs (LIMIT, non-deterministic functions, unsupported UDFs) that force FULL refresh mode; enabling incremental refresh processes smaller deltas, using far less memory. Option C (`MAX_DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0`) is a retention setting, not a memory fix — and it would not help with refresh OOM. Option D (switching to Streams+Tasks) is a valid architectural alternative but is a more disruptive change and not the most direct remediation.
questions.reference: Dynamic Table OOM — Remediation via Warehouse Sizing and Incremental Refresh

questions.stem: A data engineer uses `session.sql()` to run a SQL query and then calls `df.collect()`. What does `collect()` return?

options.text: A Snowpark DataFrame that can be further transformed
A Python list of `Row` objects containing the query results
A generator that lazily fetches rows from Snowflake
A pandas DataFrame with the query results

questions.explanation: `df.collect()` is a Snowpark action that executes the query in Snowflake and returns the complete result set as a Python list of `Row` objects to the client. Each `Row` is an immutable object with named field access (e.g., `row['column_name']`). It returns a list, not a DataFrame (A), not a generator (C — use `to_local_iterator()` for that), and not a pandas DataFrame (D — use `to_pandas()` for that).
questions.reference: Snowpark DataFrame — collect() Action

questions.stem: A data engineer is registering a permanent Snowpark Python UDF. They specify `stage_location='@my_schema.udf_stage'`. What does the `stage_location` parameter control?

options.text: The Snowflake internal stage where Snowpark uploads the UDF's Python source file and dependency artifacts for persistence
The external S3 bucket path where Snowflake reads the UDF source code at execution time
The path where Snowflake writes UDF execution logs for debugging
The stage from which Snowflake downloads package dependencies at UDF registration time

questions.explanation: When registering a permanent Snowpark UDF (`is_permanent=True`), `stage_location` specifies the Snowflake internal (or external) stage where Snowpark uploads the serialized UDF code, its Python file, and any inline imports. These artifacts are stored persistently so the UDF survives session termination and can be called by other sessions and users. Without a stage location, UDFs are temporary (session-scoped). It is not for execution logs (C) or package downloads from PyPI (D).
questions.reference: Snowpark Permanent UDFs — stage_location Parameter

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to apply a per-store machine learning model to predict demand. Each store has a different model coefficients row. The call is `df.groupBy('store_id').applyInPandas(predict_fn, output_schema)`. Select TWO TRUE statements about this pattern.

options.text: `applyInPandas` is a Snowpark method that applies a Python function to each group (partition) of data as a pandas DataFrame
Each store's partition is processed as a separate pandas DataFrame, allowing per-store model inference without cross-partition dependencies
`applyInPandas` requires the data to be sorted by `store_id` before calling `groupBy`
`applyInPandas` is not available in Snowpark; the correct approach is `groupBy().agg()` with a pandas UDF

questions.explanation: Snowpark's `groupBy(...).applyInPandas(fn, schema)` applies a Python function to each group as a pandas DataFrame — enabling per-partition (per-store) operations like loading a store-specific model and generating predictions (A and B are correct). This is analogous to PySpark's `groupBy().applyInPandas()`. Option C is wrong — pre-sorting is not required; Snowflake handles grouping internally. Option D is wrong — `applyInPandas` is a valid Snowpark API method for this use case.
questions.reference: Snowpark — groupBy().applyInPandas() for Per-Partition ML Inference

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to inspect the SQL that Snowpark will generate for a complex DataFrame transformation before executing it. They call `df.explain(extended=True)`. What additional information does `extended=True` provide compared to a plain `df.explain()`?

options.text: The estimated row count and credit cost for each plan node
Both the logical plan and the generated SQL string that Snowpark will execute
The physical execution plan including partition pruning decisions
The explain output from a real dry-run execution in Snowflake

questions.explanation: `df.explain(extended=True)` in Snowpark prints both the logical plan (the tree of Snowpark operations) AND the SQL string that Snowpark will generate and send to Snowflake. Without `extended=True`, only the logical plan is shown. Seeing the generated SQL is valuable for verifying that Snowpark is generating the intended query. Options A (credit estimates) and C (partition pruning physical plan) are not part of Snowpark's `explain()` output. Option D (dry-run execution) does not happen — `explain()` does not execute the query.
questions.reference: Snowpark DataFrame — df.explain(extended=True) for SQL Preview

questions.stem:
A data engineer writes:

```python
for row in df.to_local_iterator():
    process_row(row['customer_id'], row['amount'])
```

Compared to `df.collect()`, what is the primary advantage of `to_local_iterator()` for this pattern?

options.text: It processes rows inside Snowflake, avoiding any data transfer to the client
It streams rows to the client incrementally, so only a small buffer of rows needs to be in memory at a time
It is faster than `collect()` for small result sets
It automatically parallelizes `process_row()` across multiple CPU cores

questions.explanation: `to_local_iterator()` returns a Python iterator that fetches rows from Snowflake in chunks, rather than buffering the entire result set in memory like `collect()` does. For large result sets, this means the client only holds a small buffer of rows in RAM at any given time. Option A is wrong — data IS transferred to the client (it's a client-side iterator). Option C is a misconception — for small result sets, `collect()` may be slightly faster due to fewer round-trips. Option D is wrong — Python iterators are single-threaded; parallelizing `process_row()` would require additional concurrency libraries.
questions.reference: Snowpark — to_local_iterator() vs. collect() Memory Characteristics

questions.stem: A Snowpark Python stored procedure calls `session.sql('SELECT ...').collect()` inside its body. The procedure is registered as `EXECUTE AS OWNER`. Which privileges determine whether the SELECT succeeds?

options.text: The caller's current role privileges, since SQL inside stored procedures always uses the caller's context
The owner's role privileges, since `EXECUTE AS OWNER` means all SQL in the procedure runs with the owner's role's access rights
The SYSADMIN role's privileges, which are inherited by all stored procedures automatically
Both the caller's and owner's privileges are checked; the query fails unless both roles have SELECT access

questions.explanation: `EXECUTE AS OWNER` means the stored procedure runs all SQL statements using the privileges of the role that owns the procedure. The caller's role is not checked for the SQL inside the procedure body — only for the privilege to EXECUTE the procedure itself. This enables privileged data access patterns where callers can read/write tables through a procedure without holding direct table privileges. Option A describes `EXECUTE AS CALLER`. Option C is wrong — SYSADMIN is not auto-inherited. Option D is wrong — only the owner's privileges matter for SQL inside an `EXECUTE AS OWNER` procedure.
questions.reference: Stored Procedure EXECUTE AS OWNER — Privilege Context for SQL

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to copy all columns from a `STAGING` table into a `TARGET` table, but exclude a column named `INTERNAL_LOAD_ID` that exists in `STAGING` but should not be in `TARGET`. Which Snowflake approach achieves this without listing all other columns explicitly?

options.text: `INSERT INTO target SELECT * EXCEPT (internal_load_id) FROM staging`
`INSERT INTO target SELECT * MINUS internal_load_id FROM staging`
`INSERT INTO target SELECT * FROM staging WHERE column_name != 'internal_load_id'`
Column exclusion requires listing all columns explicitly; there is no shorthand in Snowflake SQL

questions.explanation: Snowflake supports the `SELECT * EXCEPT (col1, col2, ...)` syntax, which selects all columns from a table except the listed ones. `INSERT INTO target SELECT * EXCEPT (internal_load_id) FROM staging` is the correct and concise way to copy all columns except `INTERNAL_LOAD_ID` without listing every other column. Option B uses `MINUS` which is a set operation, not a column exclusion syntax. Option C is wrong — `WHERE column_name != ...` is a row filter, not a column filter. Option D is incorrect — Snowflake does support `EXCEPT` in SELECT.
questions.reference: SELECT * EXCEPT — Column Exclusion in Snowflake SQL

questions.stem:
A data engineer runs:

```sql
MERGE INTO products_target t
USING products_staging s
ON t.product_id = s.product_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET t.name = s.name, t.price = s.price
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (product_id, name, price) VALUES (s.product_id, s.name, s.price)
WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE;
```

The staging table has 1,000 rows. The target table has 2,000 rows, of which 800 match staging rows. How many rows are deleted by `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE`?

options.text: 200 rows — the rows in staging not in target
1,200 rows — the rows in target not in staging
800 rows — the rows that matched and were updated
0 rows — `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE` only applies when staging is empty

questions.explanation: `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE` handles rows that exist in the TARGET but have no matching row in the SOURCE (staging). The target has 2,000 rows total, 800 of which match staging rows. The remaining 2,000 - 800 = 1,200 target rows have no match in staging and are deleted by this clause. The 800 matched rows are updated by `WHEN MATCHED`. The 200 staging rows without a target match (1,000 staging - 800 matching) are inserted by `WHEN NOT MATCHED`. Option A confuses source-not-in-target (inserts) with target-not-in-source (deletes).
questions.reference: MERGE — WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE DELETE Row Count

questions.stem: A data engineer uses `GENERATOR(ROWCOUNT => 365)` combined with `DATEADD('day', SEQ4(), '2024-01-01'::DATE)` to generate a date dimension table. What does this expression produce?

options.text: 365 rows with a date column containing dates from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31
An infinite sequence of dates starting from 2024-01-01, limited by a WHERE clause
365 random dates within 2024
An error — `DATEADD` cannot be used with `SEQ4()` because SEQ4 is non-deterministic

questions.explanation: `TABLE(GENERATOR(ROWCOUNT => 365))` generates 365 rows. `SEQ4()` produces sequential integers 0, 1, 2, ..., 364 for each row. `DATEADD('day', SEQ4(), '2024-01-01'::DATE)` adds 0, 1, 2, ..., 364 days to January 1, 2024, producing dates January 1 through December 30 (365 days inclusive). This is the standard Snowflake pattern for generating a date dimension. Option B (infinite) is wrong — `ROWCOUNT` limits the rows. Option C (random) is wrong — `SEQ4()` is sequential. Option D is wrong — `DATEADD` works perfectly with `SEQ4()`.
questions.reference: GENERATOR + SEQ4() + DATEADD — Date Dimension Generation Pattern

questions.stem: A data engineer checks the `QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR` setting on a warehouse. This parameter is currently set to 0. What does a value of 0 mean?

options.text: Query Acceleration is disabled on this warehouse
Query Acceleration is enabled with no upper limit on the scale factor Snowflake can use
Query Acceleration is enabled but limited to using the same size as the current warehouse (1x scale)
Query Acceleration will only activate for queries that use 0% of the warehouse's capacity

questions.explanation: `QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR = 0` means Query Acceleration Service (QAS) is enabled with NO upper limit on the scale factor. Snowflake can use as much elastic compute as it determines necessary to accelerate the outlier query. Setting it to a positive number (e.g., 8) caps the maximum additional compute at 8x the warehouse size. Setting `ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION = FALSE` disables it entirely. A value of 0 does NOT disable it (A is wrong) and does not mean 1x (C is wrong).
questions.reference: QUERY_ACCELERATION_MAX_SCALE_FACTOR — Value of 0 Meaning

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to reuse the results of an expensive query that ran 2 hours ago, identified by its `QUERY_ID = '01a2b3c4-...'`. Which SQL expression retrieves the cached result without re-executing the query?

options.text: `SELECT * FROM TABLE(RESULT_SCAN('01a2b3c4-...'))`
`SELECT * FROM SNOWFLAKE.RESULT_CACHE WHERE QUERY_ID = '01a2b3c4-...'`
`EXECUTE USING CACHED_RESULT '01a2b3c4-...'`
`SELECT * FROM TABLE(QUERY_HISTORY()) WHERE QUERY_ID = '01a2b3c4-...'`

questions.explanation: `SELECT * FROM TABLE(RESULT_SCAN('<query_id>'))` is Snowflake's function for reading a previously executed query's result from the result cache without re-running the query. The result is available for up to 24 hours after the original query executed. Option B uses a non-existent `SNOWFLAKE.RESULT_CACHE` table. Option C uses non-existent `EXECUTE USING CACHED_RESULT` syntax. Option D (`QUERY_HISTORY()`) returns metadata about past queries, not their result sets.
questions.reference: RESULT_SCAN() — Accessing Cached Query Results by QUERY_ID

questions.stem:
A data engineer runs the following to check task run history:

```sql
SELECT *
FROM TABLE(INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TASK_HISTORY(
  TASK_NAME => 'DAILY_SILVER_LOAD',
  SCHEDULED_TIME_RANGE_START => DATEADD('day', -7, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP())
))
WHERE STATE = 'FAILED'
ORDER BY SCHEDULED_TIME DESC;
```

The query returns 0 rows even though the engineer saw failures in the Snowflake UI last week. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TASK_HISTORY()` only retains data for the last 7 days; failures from exactly 7 days ago may have aged out
The `TASK_NAME` filter is case-sensitive and must match the task's name exactly as stored in Snowflake (uppercase by default)
`TASK_HISTORY()` does not support the `TASK_NAME` parameter; it must be filtered with a WHERE clause instead
Failed task runs are not recorded in `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TASK_HISTORY()`; only SUCCEEDED runs are tracked

questions.explanation: `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TASK_HISTORY()` retains data for the last 7 days. If the engineer saw failures 7 or more days ago, those records may have aged out of the INFORMATION_SCHEMA view. For longer-term history (up to 365 days), use `SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE.TASK_HISTORY`. Option B is a possible issue but task names in INFORMATION_SCHEMA are typically case-insensitive when Snowflake stores them in uppercase by default. Option C is wrong — `TASK_NAME` IS a valid parameter for the function. Option D is wrong — failed runs are definitely recorded.
questions.reference: INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TASK_HISTORY — 7-Day Retention Limit

questions.stem: A data engineer builds a pipeline where a Snowflake Task runs a stored procedure that performs data quality checks. If the check fails, the stored procedure should cause the task to fail and stop downstream tasks from running. Which Snowpark Python construct inside the stored procedure achieves this?

options.text: `return False` — returning a falsy value from the stored procedure marks the task as failed
Raise a Python exception using `raise ValueError('DQ check failed: null_pct exceeded threshold')` — an unhandled exception causes the task to fail
`session.sql('ALTER TASK my_task SUSPEND').collect()` — suspending the task from within stops downstream processing
`CALL SYSTEM$FAIL_TASK('DQ check failed')` — a system procedure call that marks the current task as failed

questions.explanation: In a Snowpark Python stored procedure called by a task, raising an unhandled Python exception causes the stored procedure to exit with an error, which in turn marks the task run as FAILED. A FAILED task stops downstream dependent tasks from running in the current DAG cycle. Option A is wrong — returning `False` does not fail the task; only unhandled exceptions do. Option C (suspending the task from within) is overly aggressive and changes the task's schedule, not just the current run. Option D uses a non-existent `SYSTEM$FAIL_TASK` procedure.
questions.reference: Snowpark Stored Procedures — Raising Exceptions to Fail Tasks

questions.stem: A data engineer is using the Snowflake Kafka Connector to load data from a Kafka topic into Snowflake. By default, in what format does the Kafka Connector stage data before committing it to the target table?

options.text: CSV files in an internal stage, loaded via COPY INTO
Avro or JSON files in an internal stage, loaded via Snowpipe (auto-ingest) to the target table
Parquet files in an external S3 stage, loaded via COPY INTO
The Kafka Connector inserts rows directly via JDBC without staging

questions.explanation: The Snowflake Kafka Connector works by: (1) buffering incoming Kafka messages, (2) writing them as Avro or JSON files to a Snowflake internal stage, and (3) using Snowpipe to auto-ingest those staged files into the target Snowflake table. This file-based staging approach provides durability and enables efficient bulk loading. The connector does not write CSV (A), does not use external stages by default (C), and does not use direct JDBC inserts (D) — the staging+Snowpipe pattern is the connector's native mechanism.
questions.reference: Snowflake Kafka Connector — Internal Staging and Snowpipe Ingestion

questions.stem: A data engineer is designing a pipeline to ingest 10,000 small IoT device readings per second into Snowflake with end-to-end latency of under 5 seconds. Which ingestion approach is BEST suited for this requirement?

options.text: Classic Snowpipe with file-based auto-ingest from S3
A Snowflake Task scheduled every 5 minutes that bulk-loads from an external stage
Snowpipe Streaming using the `insertRows()` SDK to push rows directly without file staging
`COPY INTO` triggered by a task every 30 seconds from a landing stage

questions.explanation: Snowpipe Streaming (`insertRows()` SDK) is purpose-built for low-latency, high-throughput row insertion. It bypasses file staging entirely — the SDK sends rows directly to Snowflake's streaming endpoint, achieving sub-second to a few-second end-to-end latency. Classic Snowpipe (A) has higher latency (typically 1–3 minutes) due to file batching and staging. A 5-minute task (B) has far too much latency. `COPY INTO` every 30 seconds (C) still involves file staging overhead and 30-second minimum latency.
questions.reference: Snowpipe Streaming — Use Case for Sub-5-Second IoT Ingestion

questions.stem: A data engineer writes a MERGE statement where the source has two rows with the same key. When two source rows match the same target row, what does Snowflake do by default?

options.text: Snowflake applies the UPDATE for the first matching source row and ignores subsequent source rows for the same target row
Snowflake raises an error: 'MERGE INTO ... cannot update the same row of the target table multiple times in a single MERGE statement'
Snowflake applies both UPDATEs sequentially, with the last source row's values winning
Snowflake ignores both rows and leaves the target row unchanged when there are duplicate source matches

questions.explanation: In Snowflake, if a MERGE statement's source dataset contains multiple rows that would match the same target row (violating the merge's one-to-one assumption for matched rows), Snowflake raises a non-determinism error: 'merge into ... cannot be applied to a target table with a variable number of rows for each row of the source table.' This is a critical guard against non-deterministic updates. The developer must deduplicate the source before merging. Options A, C, and D all describe behaviors that would produce non-deterministic results, which is exactly what Snowflake prevents.
questions.reference: MERGE — Non-Determinism Error with Duplicate Source Rows

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to insert data from `source_table` into `target_table` but exclude any rows that already exist in `target_table` (identified by `record_id`). They want to avoid a MERGE and use the simplest INSERT approach. Which SQL accomplishes this?

options.text: `INSERT INTO target_table SELECT * FROM source_table EXCEPT SELECT * FROM target_table`
`INSERT INTO target_table SELECT s.* FROM source_table s LEFT JOIN target_table t ON s.record_id = t.record_id WHERE t.record_id IS NULL`
`INSERT IGNORE INTO target_table SELECT * FROM source_table`
`INSERT INTO target_table SELECT * FROM source_table WHERE record_id NOT IN (SELECT record_id FROM target_table)`

questions.explanation: Using a LEFT JOIN with a NULL filter (`WHERE t.record_id IS NULL`) is the standard Snowflake pattern for an anti-join insert — inserting only rows from the source that don't exist in the target. Option A uses `EXCEPT` which compares entire rows (all columns), not just the `record_id` key — this can miss duplicates if other columns differ. Option C uses `INSERT IGNORE` which is MySQL syntax, not valid in Snowflake. Option D (NOT IN subquery) works but can have performance issues with large target tables and NULL handling edge cases — the LEFT JOIN anti-join (B) is more robust and idiomatic.
questions.reference: Anti-Join INSERT — LEFT JOIN with NULL Filter Pattern

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to create a task that runs three steps in a specific order within a single task (not a DAG). They write a multi-statement task body. Which construct allows multiple SQL statements to execute sequentially in a single task's body?

options.text: A stored procedure called from the task body, encapsulating the three SQL statements
A Snowflake scripting block using `BEGIN ... END` syntax containing the three statements
Separating statements with semicolons in the task body (tasks natively support multi-statement bodies)
Both A and B are valid approaches

questions.explanation: Both approaches work for multi-step sequential execution within a single task: (A) calling a stored procedure that contains all three steps is a clean, encapsulated approach — the task body is a single `CALL` statement; (B) using a Snowflake scripting `BEGIN ... END` block directly in the task body allows multiple SQL statements to execute sequentially within the task. Option C is wrong — Snowflake task bodies support only a SINGLE SQL statement or a CALL to a stored procedure; you cannot separate multiple statements with semicolons in a task body. The correct approaches are A and B (hence D).
questions.reference: Task Body — Single Statement or Stored Procedure CALL

questions.stem: A data engineer has a dynamic table `REVENUE_SUMMARY` with `TARGET LAG = '1 hour'`. A critical upstream data correction is made, and the team needs `REVENUE_SUMMARY` to reflect the corrected data immediately, not in the next scheduled refresh. Which command achieves this without changing the lag setting?

options.text: `ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE revenue_summary SET TARGET LAG = '1 minute'` then revert it back after the refresh
`ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE revenue_summary REFRESH`
`CALL SYSTEM$DYNAMIC_TABLE_REFRESH('revenue_summary')`
`CREATE OR REPLACE DYNAMIC TABLE revenue_summary ...` to force an immediate rebuild

questions.explanation: `ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE <name> REFRESH` triggers an immediate on-demand refresh of the dynamic table, incorporating the latest data from base tables right now, without altering the `TARGET LAG` setting or affecting the regular schedule. After the manual refresh completes, the table continues refreshing on its normal 1-hour schedule. Option A would modify the schedule globally and require a follow-up change. Option C uses a non-existent system procedure. Option D would rebuild the entire table, causing a full-refresh compute cost and dropping/recreating the table object.
questions.reference: ALTER DYNAMIC TABLE REFRESH — On-Demand Immediate Refresh
